topic: Gandhi's Entry, Rowlatt Satyagraha & Non-Cooperation Movement (1915–1922)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly identifies the Champaran Satyagraha and its primary outcome?
options: Year: 1916; Location: Gujarat; Issue: oppressive salt tax; Outcome: Salt Act temporarily suspended
Year: 1917; Location: Bihar; Issue: tinkathia system forcing indigo cultivation; Outcome: Champaran Agrarian Act abolished the tinkathia system
Year: 1918; Location: Maharashtra; Issue: forced cotton cultivation; Outcome: revenue remission ordered
Year: 1919; Location: Punjab; Issue: land revenue during famine; Outcome: Rowlatt Act repealed
questions.explanation: The Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was Gandhi's first civil disobedience in India. It was in Champaran district, Bihar, where indigo farmers were trapped in the tinkathia system — compelled to grow indigo on 3/20ths of their land for British planters at exploitative fixed prices. Gandhi arrived, refused to leave despite a notice from the magistrate, conducted systematic fact-finding, and pressured the government. The result was the Champaran Agrarian Act 1917, which abolished the tinkathia system. It is significant as the first civil disobedience movement in India under Gandhi and established his investigative-pressure model of satyagraha.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Champaran Satyagraha (1917, Bihar, tinkathia system, Agrarian Act) is one of the most frequently tested Gandhi early-movements facts

questions.text: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on 13 April 1919. Who ordered the firing, and what was the official death toll according to the Hunter Commission?
options: General Dyer ordered firing; official death toll — 1,000 killed
Michael O'Dwyer ordered firing; official death toll — 379 killed
General Dyer ordered firing; official death toll — 379 killed
Lord Chelmsford ordered firing; official death toll — 576 killed
questions.explanation: Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer ordered his 90 troops to fire into the crowd assembled at Jallianwala Bagh on 13 April 1919 (Baisakhi festival) in Amritsar. The Hunter Commission (set up to inquire into the Punjab events of 1919) gave an official death toll of 379 with approximately 1,200 wounded. Indian estimates were higher — over 1,000 dead. Michael O'Dwyer was the Lieutenant-Governor of Punjab who approved Dyer's action; O'Dwyer was NOT present at the firing. Udham Singh later shot and killed O'Dwyer in London in 1940. General Dyer was censured by the Hunter Commission and forced to resign from the army, but the British House of Lords praised him and the public raised £26,000 for him.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Jallianwala Bagh (Dyer ordered firing, 379 official dead, Hunter Commission) and the Dyer vs. O'Dwyer distinction are the two most tested traps in this topic

questions.text: At which INC session was the Non-Cooperation Movement formally and fully adopted, and who presided over that session?
options: Calcutta Special Session, September 1920 — presided by Lala Lajpat Rai
Nagpur Session, December 1920 — presided by C. Vijayaraghavachariar
Amritsar Session, December 1919 — presided by Motilal Nehru
Lucknow Session, 1916 — presided by Ambika Charan Majumdar
questions.explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement's history involves TWO sessions: (1) Calcutta Special Session, September 1920 — Lala Lajpat Rai presided; Gandhi proposed the NCM; it was debated and initially adopted. (2) Nagpur Session, December 1920 — C. Vijayaraghavachariar presided; the NCM was formally and overwhelmingly adopted with a broader mandate; Congress also reorganised itself on linguistic lines and adopted 'Swaraj' as its goal. When exam questions say NCM was 'adopted' and give one answer, they usually expect Nagpur 1920. But if the question says 'first proposed' or 'special session,' the answer is Calcutta 1920. The Amritsar Session 1919 (Motilal Nehru) was significant for passing the Congress's condemnation of Jallianwala Bagh — it was NOT the NCM session.
questions.source: SSC CGL — NCM adoption (Calcutta special 1920 vs. Nagpur 1920) is a consistently tested multi-step identification; both sessions and their presidents are testable

questions.text: What was the immediate cause that led Gandhi to withdraw the Non-Cooperation Movement in February 1922?
options: The British agreed to repeal the Rowlatt Act, removing the primary grievance
Violence at Chauri Chaura, Gorakhpur (UP) — a mob burned a police station, killing 22 policemen
The Khilafat Movement collapsed when Atatürk abolished the Caliphate, removing Muslim support
Gandhi was arrested before he could continue, and the leaderless movement fell apart on its own
questions.explanation: On 4-5 February 1922, at Chauri Chaura in Gorakhpur district (United Provinces), a procession of Non-Cooperation volunteers clashed with police. The police fired; the mob retaliated by burning the police station, killing 22 (some accounts say 23) policemen inside. When Gandhi heard of the violence, he announced the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement on 12 February 1922 — unilaterally, without consulting the Congress Working Committee. His reasoning: the masses were not ready for truly non-violent satyagraha, and he would rather call off the movement than allow violence to corrupt it. This decision shocked nationalist leaders including Nehru, C.R. Das, and Subhas Bose. Gandhi was subsequently arrested in March 1922 and sentenced to 6 years (served 2 years).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Chauri Chaura (February 1922, Gorakhpur, 22 police killed) as the immediate cause of NCM withdrawal is one of the most tested cause-and-effect facts in Modern History

questions.text: Udham Singh assassinated someone in London in 1940 to avenge the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. Who exactly did he kill, and why that person rather than General Dyer?
options: He killed General Reginald Dyer because Dyer had personally ordered the firing
He killed Michael O'Dwyer (Lieutenant-Governor of Punjab) because O'Dwyer had approved Dyer's action; Dyer had already died of natural causes in 1927
He killed Lord Chelmsford (Viceroy at the time of Jallianwala Bagh) in the British Parliament
He killed Lord Birkenhead (Secretary of State for India) who had refused to prosecute Dyer
questions.explanation: Udham Singh shot and killed Michael O'Dwyer at Caxton Hall, London, on 13 March 1940 — on the anniversary of O'Dwyer's approval of martial law in Punjab. O'Dwyer had been the Lieutenant-Governor of Punjab and had approved General Dyer's actions at Jallianwala Bagh, even praised Dyer's response. Udham Singh's logic: Dyer was the man who pulled the trigger, but O'Dwyer was the man who authorised and celebrated it. General Dyer had already died of natural causes in 1927 (after suffering a series of strokes), so he was no longer available as a target. Udham Singh was hanged at Pentonville Prison, London, on 31 July 1940 — the same prison where Madanlal Dhingra had been hanged 31 years earlier. Udham Singh is also known as Muhammad Singh Azad (his chosen name).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Udham Singh question routinely mixes up Dyer vs. O'Dwyer (the person who gave the order vs. the person who approved it); the fact that Dyer had already died of natural causes in 1927 is an important detail that eliminates option A

questions.text: The Khilafat Movement ended in 1924 — but NOT because the British yielded to Indian Muslim demands. What actually ended it?
options: The British government agreed to restore the Ottoman Caliphate under international pressure from Muslim nations
Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, the nationalist leader of Turkey, abolished the Caliphate himself in March 1924, removing the institution the movement was fighting to protect
Mohammed Ali and Shaukat Ali abandoned the movement after being offered positions in the colonial government
Gandhi withdrew his support from the Khilafat Movement after communal riots broke out in 1921, causing it to collapse
questions.explanation: The Khilafat Movement's ironic end: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk — the Turkish nationalist hero who had defeated the Greeks and reclaimed Turkey — abolished the Sultanate in November 1922 and the Caliphate in March 1924. The very institution (the Caliphate) that Indian Muslims had been demanding be preserved was eliminated by a Turk, not a Briton. This made the Khilafat demand instantly meaningless — there was no longer a Caliph to protect. The movement collapsed abruptly. The Hindu-Muslim unity that Gandhi had so carefully built through the Khilafat-NCM alliance shattered; communal riots increased in the mid-1920s. Gandhi's strategy of using Khilafat grievances as a bridge for unity thus came to an end not through political failure but through an unexpected event in Turkey.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because students know 'Khilafat ended' but not why; the Atatürk-abolished-the-Caliphate fact is the key; the strategic implication (end of Hindu-Muslim unity) is the analytical point tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Gandhi's fast during the Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) is considered unusual. Why?
options: It was unusual because it was Gandhi's first ever fast — he had never fasted as a political tool before this moment

It was unusual because Gandhi was fasting not against the British but against the mill workers he was trying to help — to prevent them from breaking the strike; and because one of the mill owners was the brother of Gandhi's own associate Anasuya Sarabhai
It was unusual because Gandhi declared he would fast for 21 days, the longest fast in Indian political history
It was unusual because the British government ordered Gandhi arrested mid-fast, creating an international incident
questions.explanation: The Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) was unusual in two respects: (1) Gandhi's fast was directed at the workers themselves — not at the British, not at the mill owners, but at the strikers who were on the verge of breaking the strike out of hunger. He told them: 'I will fast until you maintain your resolve.' This self-directed pressure was a novel application of satyagraha's logic — he was creating moral pressure on the people he was helping, not on the opponent. (2) The situation was personally awkward because Anasuya Sarabhai, Gandhi's close associate and one of India's first trade union leaders, was on the workers' side — but her own brother, Ambalal Sarabhai, was the mill owner being struck against. Gandhi was thus positioned between two people he personally knew and cared about. The mill owners eventually agreed to arbitration; workers received a 35% wage hike.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) is tested for the Anasuya Sarabhai detail and for what makes Gandhi's fast unusual (against strikers, not against opponent)

questions.text: The Swaraj Party was formed in 1923 after the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement. Who founded it and what was its strategy?
options: Formed by Gandhi and Nehru to continue NCM through parliamentary obstruction
Formed by C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru to enter legislative councils and obstruct governance from within, since mass movement was suspended
Formed by Subhas Chandra Bose and Lala Lajpat Rai as a revolutionary alternative to Congress
Formed by Rajagopalachari to oppose Gandhi's withdrawal and continue the Non-Cooperation Movement
questions.explanation: After Gandhi withdrew the NCM in February 1922, the nationalist movement split into two camps: (1) 'No-Changers' — Rajagopalachari, Vallabhbhai Patel — who wanted to continue constructive work and oppose council entry as a corruption of non-cooperation; (2) 'Swarajists' (Pro-Changers) — C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru — who argued that since mass movement was suspended, nationalists should enter the legislative councils and obstruct British governance from within (a strategy of 'wrecking from within'). C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the Swaraj Party in December 1922 / January 1923. They contested the 1923 elections, won seats, and were remarkably effective — blocking the budget, passing no-confidence motions, making legislative work ungovernable. C.R. Das was the Bengal Congress president; Subhas Chandra Bose became his Bengal secretary and protégé.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know the NCM was withdrawn but rarely know what happened immediately after; the Swaraj Party (C.R. Das + Motilal Nehru, 1923, council entry strategy) appears in integrated questions about the 1920s Congress

questions.text: What was the 'crawling order' issued by General Dyer, and in what context was it given?
options: Dyer ordered all soldiers to crawl through the narrow lane into Jallianwala Bagh in a military formation to avoid being seen

After Jallianwala Bagh, Dyer ordered that Indians passing through a particular street in Amritsar (where British woman Miss Sherwood had been attacked) must crawl on all fours; violators were flogged publicly
The 'crawling order' was Dyer's order to the crowd at Jallianwala Bagh to crawl to safety, which they misunderstood, leading to the stampede
Dyer ordered Indian prisoners at Lahore Central Jail to crawl as a punishment for participating in anti-Rowlatt protests
questions.explanation: Before the Jallianwala Bagh massacre, Miss Marcella Sherwood (a British missionary and teacher) had been attacked by a mob in a narrow street (Kucha Kurrichhan) in Amritsar. After taking command, General Dyer issued several martial law orders including the 'crawling order': Indians were required to crawl on all fours (on their hands and knees) through the entire length of this street. Any Indian seen walking upright through it was arrested and flogged publicly. The Hunter Commission specifically condemned the crawling order as gratuitous humiliation. The order became a symbol of Dyer's intent to degrade Indians — not just restore order — and is cited alongside the Bagh massacre as evidence of systematic racial contempt. The crawling order was less deadly than the massacre but, for many Indians, more psychologically damaging because it was calculated humiliation.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Jallianwala Bagh question usually focuses on the massacre; the 'crawling order' is a related fact about Dyer's broader conduct that is tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Moplah Rebellion of 1921 is often discussed in connection with the Non-Cooperation and Khilafat movements. What was it, and why was it problematic for the nationalist alliance?
options: A peasant rebellion in Malabar (Kerala) by Muslim farmers (Moplahs) that mixed Khilafat grievances with anti-landlord fury; it turned communal, with some Hindus forcibly converted, straining Hindu-Muslim unity in the NCM-Khilafat alliance
A Ghadar-inspired revolt in Malabar by Punjabi immigrants who had settled there after the Ghadar Party's 1915 failure
A British-sponsored uprising designed to divide Hindu and Muslim nationalists by instigating communal violence
A tribal rebellion in the forests of Malabar against forest reservation laws, incorrectly labelled 'Moplah' by British authorities
questions.explanation: The Moplah Rebellion (August-December 1921) was an uprising by Moplah (Mappila) Muslims — peasant farmers and landless labourers — in the Malabar region of present-day Kerala. Their grievances were dual: (1) Economic/agrarian — they were largely tenant farmers under oppressive Hindu landlords (Jenmi system); (2) Religious/political — Khilafat agitation had given them a sense of Islamic solidarity and a language for resistance. The rebellion quickly turned violent and communal: mosques were attacked by British forces; Moplahs attacked and burned temples; some Hindus were forcibly converted. The British suppressed it brutally (the 'Moplah Van' incident — prisoners suffocated in a closed railway wagon). It damaged the Khilafat-Congress alliance because Congress leaders were torn between condemning the violence and alienating Muslim participants. The rebellion is historically contested: for some, it was an agrarian revolt; for others, a communal episode; for British administrators, a useful justification for separating Hindu-Muslim agitation.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Moplah Rebellion appears in textbooks as a one-line mention but its significance (communal turn within a supposedly unified NCM-Khilafat movement) is tested in analytical questions

questions.text:
Match the satyagraha campaign with its location and key issue:
1. Champaran Satyagraha (1917)    A. Gujarat — peasants; revenue remission during crop failure
2. Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918)   B. Bihar — indigo farmers; tinkathia system
3. Kheda Satyagraha (1918)        C. Gujarat — mill workers; wage increase
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Champaran Satyagraha (1917) → B. Bihar — indigo farmers trapped in the tinkathia system (forced to grow indigo on 3/20ths of land); outcome: Champaran Agrarian Act 1917. (2) Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) → C. Gujarat (Ahmedabad city) — mill workers demanded 50% wage hike during plague epidemic; Gandhi fasted; workers got 35% via arbitration. (3) Kheda Satyagraha (1918) → A. Gujarat (Kheda district) — peasants refused to pay land revenue because crops had failed below the 25% threshold that should trigger remission; government quietly backed down. All three are in Gujarat/Bihar; a common distractor is to swap Ahmedabad (urban, mill workers) with Kheda (rural, peasants). Vallabhbhai Patel emerged in Kheda, not Champaran.
questions.source: Three-way matching of Gandhi's early satyagrahas (Champaran/Bihar/tinkathia, Ahmedabad/mills/wages, Kheda/peasants/revenue) is the single most frequently tested question pattern for Gandhi's pre-NCM period

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Non-Cooperation Movement is INCORRECT?
options: It was first proposed at the Calcutta Special Session (September 1920) presided over by Lala Lajpat Rai
The programme included boycott of government educational institutions, courts, councils, and foreign goods
Gandhi withdrew the movement after the Chauri Chaura incident in which a police station was burned and 22 policemen were killed in Gorakhpur district
Gandhi was sentenced to 10 years' imprisonment by Judge Broomfield after the withdrawal of NCM
questions.explanation: The INCORRECT statement is option D. Gandhi was sentenced to SIX years (not 10) by Judge Broomfield in March 1922. Broomfield famously expressed personal regret at having to sentence Gandhi, saying he would be pleased if the government could release him early. Gandhi served only 2 years; he was released in February 1924 for an appendix operation ordered by his doctors. Options A, B, and C are all correct: NCM was first proposed at Calcutta Special Session September 1920 (Lajpat Rai presided) (A); the boycott programme covered government schools, courts, councils, and foreign goods (B); Chauri Chaura (Gorakhpur district, UP) — 22 police killed in burning station — was the immediate trigger for Gandhi's unilateral withdrawal in February 1922 (C).
questions.source: INCORRECT statement format for NCM details (Gandhi's sentence = 6 years not 10) is a standard SSC trap; the other three options are consistently tested facts used as true distractors

questions.text: Who was the first leader to coin or articulate the 'Safety Valve theory' about the Indian National Congress — and what does this theory claim? (This connects to the pre-NCM context of Gandhi inheriting Congress.)
options: Bal Gangadhar Tilak — the theory claimed that Congress was a safety valve to prevent genuine revolt by channelling nationalism into harmless petitions
Lala Lajpat Rai — the theory claimed that Congress was created by A.O. Hume as a safety valve to divert nationalist energies from revolution into manageable constitutional demands
Bipin Chandra Pal — the theory claimed that Congress was a safety valve for British commercial interests in India
Gandhi — the theory claimed that Non-Cooperation was a safety valve to prevent violent revolution by offering a disciplined non-violent alternative
questions.explanation: The Safety Valve theory was articulated by Lala Lajpat Rai. The theory: A.O. Hume (a retired British ICS officer who co-founded the INC in 1885) created Congress as a 'safety valve' — to give educated Indians a forum for constitutional expression, thereby preventing their frustrated nationalism from turning into violent revolution. Modern historians argue this is overly cynical about Hume's motives and ignores how Congress became genuinely radical under Gandhi's leadership. Regardless of its truth, the Safety Valve theory is a standard SSC question because it challenges the nationalist 'freedom struggle' narrative with a more cynical British-strategy explanation. Lajpat Rai's name and the 'safety valve' phrase are the two testable facts; don't confuse it with any other leader.
questions.source: Safety Valve theory (Lala Lajpat Rai, about A.O. Hume's founding of Congress) is a frequently asked crossover question between the Moderate Phase topic and the overall Congress founding narrative — tested as both author-identification and concept-explanation
